narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school journey, including your undergraduate studies and LSAT preparation. The admissions model we use evaluates applicants based on patterns from past students' paths to the bar exam, predicting whether someone is likely to pass on the first try. For you, it estimated a 40% chance of failing the bar exam, which unfortunately crossed our threshold for denial, as we prioritize admitting those with the strongest projected odds of success to maintain our program's high standards. The most influential factor pulling in a positive direction—toward predicting you'd pass—was your LSAT score of 35, which stands strong just below the typical applicant's 36.7 and signaled solid analytical skills to the model. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of around 5.5, emerged as the top concern, strongly pushing the prediction toward failure by highlighting a dip in later academic performance compared to peers. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, lower than the typical 3.21, also nudged the odds downward, though less forcefully, suggesting room for growth from your earlier Year 1 decile of 1. Meanwhile, your upper-middle family income provided a helpful boost toward passing, likely reflecting access to resources that aid success. Being in the part-time work category, which is uncommon (only about 7% of students), added a slight push toward the failure prediction, differing from the full-time norm of most applicants. Overall, your profile compares to typical students as competitive in test scores and background but held back by these relative weaknesses in grades and study mode. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a person or your dedication—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push it higher, strengthening your later-year academics through targeted courses or tutoring, and exploring full-time program options if feasible. We encourage you to reapply after addressing these areas, and our admissions team is here to discuss personalized strategies for boosting your bar exam readiness. Keep pursuing your goals—you've got this.
